perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted according to the laws of the Republic of South Africa, without reference to the choice of laws' provisions of the Republic of South Africa.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted according to principles of equity and fairness.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to the laws of the Republic of South Africa and replacing it with 'principles of equity and fairness,' the contract attempts to bypass legally mandated protections afforded by South African law, introducing legal ambiguity and potentially disadvantaging one of the parties. This is because statutory law provides concrete rights and remedies that equitable principles may not fully encompass.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Legality and Enforceability
perturbation.law_citation: Section 89 of the Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 89 of the Consumer Protection Act (CPA) outlines the role of the National Consumer Tribunal in ensuring fair and lawful contractual terms. Substituting the governing law with vague principles of 'equity and fairness' undermines the CPA's specific consumer protections and the Tribunal's oversight, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the governing law of the Republic of South Africa with 'principles of equity and fairness.' This omission contradicts the legal obligation to adhere to South African law, potentially undermining legally mandated consumer protections. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this bypass of legally mandated protections afforded by South African law, and the Law Explanation indicates that this change undermines the CPA's specific consumer protections. Since the original text ensured the contract was governed by a specific legal framework (South African law), and the changed text replaces this with a much more ambiguous and less defined standard ('principles of equity and fairness'), this constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction, as it removes the legally mandated consumer protection that adherence to South African law provides.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this agreement, monetary damages would not be an adequate remedy and that the plaintiff part shall be entitled to injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction and be reimbursed for any costs, claims, demands or liabilities arising directly or indirectly out of a breach. Nothing contained in this agreement shall be construed as prohibiting a party or its affiliate from pursuing any other remedies available to it for a breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this agreement, monetary damages would be the sole and exclusive remedy available to the plaintiff party.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the remedy for breach to only monetary damages, the modified text removes the possibility of injunctive relief. This could be problematic under South African law if the breach involves confidential information where monetary damages alone may not adequately compensate for the harm caused. Injunctive relief is a standard remedy for breach of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law of Contract - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Under South African contract law, remedies for breach include specific performance and interdictory (injunctive) relief. By limiting remedies to monetary damages only, the modified contract contradicts the established legal principle that a party is entitled to seek specific performance or an interdict to prevent ongoing harm, particularly in cases involving confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: A Reasonable Expectation Of Privacy - Privacy Protection - Canada ARTICLE 28 March 2019 A Reasonable Expectation Of Privacy CW Clark Wilson LLP More Contributor Clark Wilson is a multifaceted law firm based in Vancouver, BC with a strong track record of being highly integrated into our clients’ businesses. Known for our industry insight, entrepreneurial culture and strategic networks, we actively seek to connect our clients with the people, resources and solutions they need to succeed. Explore Firm Details One dissenting judge found that the students did have a reasonable expectation of privacy. Canada Privacy Andrea Raso and Catherine Repel Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The Supreme Court of Canada's recent decision in Jarvis provides a new framework for determining whether an individual has a reasonable expectation of privacy. Although Jarvis is a criminal case, the analysis will undoubtedly be applied in civil contexts for determining whether a breach of privacy has been committed. Ryan Jarvis was a high school teacher in Ontario who was caught making recordings of female students (particularly of their chests) while at school. The recordings were made by Jarvis using a camera pen. Jarvis was charged under the voyeurism offence in s. 162(1)(c) of the Criminal Code , which requires proof that the recording was done surreptitiously in circumstances that give rise to a reasonable expectation of privacy and that the recording was done for a sexual purpose. At trial, the judge found that the students had a reasonable expectation of privacy, but he could not find beyond a reasonable doubt that the recording was done for a sexual purpose. On appeal, the Ontario Court of Appeal found that while the recording was done for a sexual purpose, but the female students did not have a reasonable expectation of privacy as the recordings were done in common areas of the school where they would expect others could see them and where they knew they were being recorded by the school's security cameras. One dissenting judge found that the students did have a reasonable expectation of privacy. The Crown appealed to the Supreme Court of Canada (SCC) on the issue of whether the students had a reasonable expectation of privacy. The SCC unanimously found that the students had a reasonable expectation of privacy and convicted Mr. Jarvis. In the decision, the SCC confirmed that, in short, privacy is not an "all-or-nothing" concept, meaning that just because a person is in a public or semi-public space does not automatically mean that there is no expectation of privacy. Rather, determining whether an individual has a reasonable expectation of privacy is a contextual assessment that must take into account the totality of the circumstances. The SCC developed a list of considerations that will be useful in undertaking this contextual assessment, though not every consideration will be relevant in every case and there may be others: the location where the observation or recording occurred; the nature of the impugned conduct: whether it consisted of observation or recording; the awareness or consent of the person who was observed or recorded; the manner in which the observation or recording was done; the subject matter or content of the observation or recording; any rules, regulations, or policies that governed the observation or recording in question; the relationship between the person who was observed or recorded and the person who did the observation or recording; the purpose for which the observation or recording was done; and the personal attributes of the person who was observed or recorded, that is, whether the person was a child or young person. As a result of this case, we recommend that every organization revisit their policies and rules regarding cameras and recordings, including storage and retention guidelines. If a policy or rule presumes that any and all recordin
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for injunctive relief in the event of a breach, while the changed text limits the remedy to only monetary damages. The Law Explanation states that under South African contract law, remedies for breach include specific performance and interdictory (injunctive) relief and that limiting remedies to monetary damages contradicts this principle. While the snippets provided do not directly confirm South African law, the removal of injunctive relief, a standard remedy, particularly in cases involving confidential information, creates a legal contradiction, undermining the ability to seek specific performance or an interdict to prevent ongoing harm, as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Any documentation or written record or other material containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into the possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential information of FP. The SP shall, on request, and in any event if the discussions referred to in 2.1 above should not result in an agreement, return to FP all of FP's confidential information which is in physical form (including all copies) and shall destroy any other records (including, without limitation, those in machine readable form) as far as they contain FP's confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Any documentation or written record or other material containing confidential information (in whatsoever form) that comes into the possession of the SP shall itself be deemed to form part of the confidential information of FP. The SP shall, on request, return to FP all of FP's confidential information which is in physical form (including all copies).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to destroy other records (including machine-readable forms) that contain FP's confidential information if discussions do not result in an agreement, the modified contract fails to provide adequate protection for FP's confidential information. This leaves open the possibility that the SP could retain and potentially misuse the information even after discussions have ceased.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Common Law - Duty of Confidentiality
perturbation.law_citation: High Court case Waste Want (Pty) Ltd v Peters
perturbation.law_explanation: South African common law imposes a duty of confidentiality, particularly where confidential information is disclosed for a specific purpose. The case Waste Want (Pty) Ltd v Peters demonstrates the court's emphasis on protecting confidential information. By removing the destruction requirement, the modified contract weakens the protection afforded to FP's confidential information and may contravene the common law duty of confidentiality.

The appellantâs wife, as first plaintiff, instituted an action in the Cape High Court against the respondents, respectively the general medical practitioner and specialist obstetrician and gynaecologist whom she consulted during her pregnancy, for her special damages relating to the maintenance, special schooling, past and future medical expenses consequent upon her sonâs condition. The appellant, as second plaintiff, on behalf of his minor son, instituted a delictual claim in the alternative to that of the first plaintiff for the same damages. It is acknowledged that the main claim would be good in law, if it is still enforceable, and thus the same damages now claimed would be recoverable by the childâs parents.Â The respondents excepted to the appellantâs claim, which was upheld by Louw J, who dismissed the appellantâs claim with costs. With the leave of that court the matter came on appeal to this court. [2]Â Â Â Â Â Â Â In the particulars of claim it is alleged that the respondents, whilst treating the first plaintiff during her pregnancy, were under a duty to detect any abnormalities in the foetus, to advise the first plaintiff thereof, who would have undergone a termination of pregnancy and consequently that Brian would not have been born and would not have suffered from the severe physical handicaps that he does. The second respondent alleged in his exception that the appellantâs claim is âbad i
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the SP to destroy records containing FP's confidential information if an agreement is not reached. The modified text removes this obligation. According to the Law Explanation, this weakens the protection of FP's confidential information, potentially contravening common law duty of confidentiality. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the need to destroy confidential information, the removal of this clause in the modified text directly reduces the protection of confidential information, thus contradicting the common law duty of confidentiality as described in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that the removal of this destruction requirement could lead to misuse of confidential information. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall commence on the date of signature of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force indefinitely thereafter (and, in any event, beyond the term of this agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall commence on the date of signature of this agreement by the last party to sign the agreement and shall remain in force for a period of one year.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the duration of the agreement to one year, the modified text significantly reduces the protection afforded to FP's confidential information. South African law recognizes that confidentiality obligations can extend beyond the termination of a business relationship, especially where trade secrets or sensitive information are involved. A one-year limitation may be insufficient to protect FP's interests.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Law - Protection of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: While South Africa lacks a specific statute for trade secrets, the Competition Act 89 of 1998 can be used to protect confidential information that qualifies as a trade secret. By limiting the agreement to one year, the modified contract weakens the long-term protection necessary for genuine trade secrets, potentially undermining FP's ability to enforce its rights under the Competition Act or common law principles relating to the misappropriation of confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the agreement remains in force indefinitely, providing long-term protection for FP's confidential information. The changed text limits this protection to one year. The perturbation explanation specifies the potential inadequacy of a one-year limitation in safeguarding FP's interests, especially trade secrets. The law explanation highlights that the Competition Act 89 of 1998 and common law principles are used to protect confidential information, indicating long-term protection is needed for trade secrets. Since the changed text weakens the necessary long-term protection for trade secrets, it creates a legal contradiction, especially given the Omission Legal Contradiction definition focuses on the removal of essential information affecting legal mandates or protections.
